title: The Conversation You've Been Avoiding
paragraphs: You know the conversation. You’ve been rehearsing it for weeks. Maybe longer. It lives somewhere between your chest and your throat — too heavy to swallow, too risky to speak. You draft the opening line while you’re driving. You practise it in the shower. You think about it at 2am when the house is quiet and you can’t sleep because the thing that’s broken is louder than the silence.
And then the moment comes. They’re right there. Across the table. On the other end of the couch. And you say… nothing.
You smile. You move on. You tell yourself next time.
But next time never comes, does it?
And the thing that could have been a twenty-minute conversation becomes a twenty-year wall. Not because you didn’t care. Not because you didn’t know. But because you didn’t know how.

This paper is for the person who’s decided that next time is now. You’ve made the decision. Good. That decision took more courage than most people will ever muster in a lifetime. But here’s what I need you to hear: courage alone won’t get you through the door. Courage gets you to the front porch. Strategy gets you inside.

Because here’s the thing nobody tells you. It’s not just about having the conversation. It’s about having it with the right approach, in the right sequence, with the right understanding of who you’re talking to. Every human being responds to confrontation differently. And if you don’t understand the person on the other side of the table, you’ll either blow the door off the hinges or you’ll stand on the porch forever. Neither gets you where you need to go.

So I’m going to give you a framework. It’s built on a simple picture: approaching someone in conflict is like approaching their house. There are rooms. There’s a sequence. There are rules. And who answers the door determines everything about how you move through it.
